description: One of Nigeria's greatest soups. Ground melon seeds fried or cooked in a rich palm oil and pepper base with assorted meat and fish. Dense, nutty, deeply savoury. The kind of soup that makes swallow disappear.

ingredients.name: ground egusi (melon seeds)
ingredients.note: Buy already ground or grind whole seeds yourself. The finer the grind, the smoother the egusi lumps. Coarse grind gives chunkier texture — both are valid.

ingredients.name: palm oil
ingredients.note: Do not substitute. The combination of egusi and palm oil is fundamental to this soup. Use good quality — reddish-orange, not dark brown.

ingredients.name: beef or assorted meat
ingredients.note: Parboil with seasoning first, fry to colour, set aside. Ponmo, tripe, and beef chunks all work well together here.

ingredients.name: stockfish (okporoko)
ingredients.note: Soak in boiling water for 30 minutes to soften. Debone. Essential for authentic depth — hard to replicate.

ingredients.name: smoked fish
ingredients.note: Debone and flake. Adds a smoky, savoury layer.

ingredients.name: fresh or frozen spinach
ingredients.note: Or bitter leaf (ewuro) — washed thoroughly and squeezed to reduce bitterness. Or pumpkin leaf (ugwu). Add at the very end.

ingredients.name: plum tomatoes
ingredients.note: Part of the pepper base. Not as dominant as in stew — this is background flavour.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: Egusi carries heat well. 2 is a good starting point.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 blended into the pepper base, 1 finely chopped to mix with the egusi.

ingredients.name: ground crayfish
ingredients.note: Crayfish is as important as egusi itself in this soup. Do not reduce the quantity.

ingredients.name: locust beans (iru/dawadawa)
ingredients.note: Rinse briefly. Adds fermented depth that takes the soup from good to extraordinary.

ingredients.name: meat stock
ingredients.note: From parboiling the meat. The richer the stock, the better the soup.

ingredients.name: seasoning cubes
ingredients.note: Crush before adding. Season in stages.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season at the end — smoked fish and stockfish contribute salt.

steps.title: Prepare the Meats and Stock
steps.instruction: This step sets the foundation — every component needs to be fully prepped and ready before you start the soup itself. Season the beef and any assorted meats generously with curry powder, thyme, a crushed seasoning cube, roughly chopped onion, and salt, then parboil in minimal water until about 75% tender. Reserve every drop of the stock — use as little water as possible during parboiling so the resulting stock is concentrated and intensely flavoured. Fry the parboiled meat in hot oil until deeply golden-brown and set aside. For the stockfish, pour boiling water over it and leave to soak for 30 minutes until it softens enough to pull apart easily, then debone and break into large pieces. Debone the smoked fish and break into generous flakes, checking carefully for small bones.
micro_steps: Season all meat generously with curry powder, thyme, 1 crushed seasoning cube, roughly chopped onion, and salt
Place in a pot and add just enough water to barely cover — about 2cm above the meat
Parboil over medium-high heat until 75% tender — beef about 35–40 minutes, tripe 60–70 minutes
Remove meat with a slotted spoon and reserve all the stock in a bowl
Heat a generous splash of oil in a pan over high heat until shimmering
Fry the parboiled meat in batches until deeply golden-brown — 3–4 minutes per side
Set the fried meat aside on a plate
Pour boiling water over the stockfish and leave to soak for 30 minutes
Drain the stockfish, pull apart carefully, and remove all bones — keep in large pieces
Run your fingers through the smoked fish to locate and remove all bones, then break into large flakes
Have all proteins organised on a plate and ready before starting the soup
prep_notes: Start the stockfish soaking first — it needs 30 minutes in boiling water before it is soft enough to debone
Use the absolute minimum water when parboiling the meat — concentrated stock is one of the most important ingredients in this soup
Blend the pepper base (tomatoes, tatashe, scotch bonnet, 1 onion) and set it aside before turning on the heat for the soup
Mix the ground egusi with the finely chopped second onion and a small splash of water to form a thick paste — have this ready before the palm oil goes on
steps.sensory_cue: Fried meat should be deeply golden-brown and smell intensely savoury. The reserved stock should be dark, rich, and almost viscous — a sign it is properly concentrated. Stockfish after soaking should pull apart and debone without resistance; if it still feels hard, give it another 10 minutes in the hot water.
steps.failure_risk: Weak, watery stock produces a weak, thin egusi soup that no amount of seasoning can fully rescue. Using minimal water when parboiling is the single most impactful thing you can do for the quality of the finished soup. Resist the urge to add more water to speed up the parboil.

steps.title: Blend the Pepper Base and Prepare Egusi
steps.instruction: Blend the plum tomatoes, tatashe, scotch bonnet, and 1 onion together until completely smooth — egusi soup does not need or benefit from a chunky pepper base, so blend until there are no visible pieces remaining. Set this aside. In a separate bowl, finely chop or grate the second onion and add it directly into the ground egusi along with just enough water to bring it together into a thick, rough paste — think peanut butter consistency, not batter. The paste should hold its shape when pressed. This egusi-onion mixture is what goes into the hot palm oil in the next step.
micro_steps: Place plum tomatoes, tatashe, scotch bonnet, and 1 roughly chopped onion into a blender
Blend until completely smooth — no visible chunks
Pour the blended pepper into a bowl and set aside
Finely chop or grate the second onion into fine pieces
Add the chopped onion to the 3 cups of ground egusi in a bowl
Add water one tablespoon at a time, mixing as you go
Stop adding water when the egusi comes together into a thick, rough paste — like stiff peanut butter
The paste should hold its shape when you press it — not runny, not dry and crumbly
If using bitter leaf, wash it thoroughly under cold water and squeeze firmly between your hands several times to reduce bitterness — set aside
steps.sensory_cue: The blended pepper should be smooth, vibrant red-orange, and smell fresh and slightly sharp. The egusi paste with onion should smell nutty and slightly raw — like ground seeds mixed with fresh onion. The paste should look pale yellow and hold its form when pressed with a spoon.
steps.failure_risk: Adding too much water to the egusi paste creates a loose, runny mixture that splatters dangerously when it hits the hot palm oil and also fries unevenly. The paste must be thick enough to hold its shape. If you accidentally add too much water, mix in more dry ground egusi to stiffen it back up.

steps.title: Heat the Palm Oil and Fry Aromatics
steps.instruction: Pour the palm oil into your largest, heaviest pot and place over medium heat. Let it melt and heat until it becomes a clear, fluid orange — do not let it get hot enough to smoke or darken, as burnt palm oil has a harsh, unpleasant flavour that cannot be corrected. If using locust beans, add them now and fry for 1 minute, stirring — the smell will be pungent and funky, which is correct. Add the ground crayfish and stir for 30 seconds until it releases its sharp savoury aroma into the oil. Pour in the blended pepper base and increase to medium-high heat. Fry the pepper base, stirring every 90 seconds, for 10–15 minutes until it visibly reduces, deepens in colour, and the palm oil begins rising to the surface.
micro_steps: Pour ½ cup of palm oil into your largest, heaviest pot
Place over medium heat and watch carefully — heat until the oil melts and becomes clear orange
Do not let the oil smoke or turn dark at any point — reduce heat if it starts to darken
If using locust beans, rinse them briefly under cold water then add to the oil — fry for 1 minute, stirring
Add 3 tablespoons of ground crayfish and stir immediately for 30 seconds
The crayfish will release a sharp, savoury, oceanic smell into the oil — this is correct
Pour in all the blended pepper base
Increase heat to medium-high and stir everything together
Fry the pepper base, stirring every 90 seconds, for 10–15 minutes
The base is ready when it has visibly reduced, darkened, and the palm oil is clearly floating on the surface
steps.sensory_cue: The palm oil when correctly heated should be a clear, fluid orange — not dark, not smoking. After frying, the pepper base should look reduced, darker, and the palm oil should be visibly separated and floating on the surface. The combination of crayfish and locust beans in palm oil creates a deeply savoury, pungent, complex aroma that is distinctive to this soup.
steps.failure_risk: Overheated or burnt palm oil is the most common egusi soup mistake and one of the hardest to recover from — the harshness permeates the entire soup. Keep the heat firmly at medium throughout this stage and watch the oil constantly. If it starts to smoke or go dark at any point, remove the pot from the heat immediately for 30 seconds before continuing.

steps.title: Fry the Egusi
steps.instruction: Add the entire egusi paste into the fried pepper base and stir once to combine everything into the palm oil and pepper. Now stop stirring and leave the egusi undisturbed for 3 minutes — let it sit against the hot base and begin to fry, forming a slightly crusted, golden layer at the bottom of the pot. After 3 minutes, stir firmly and break up the crust. Leave it undisturbed again for 2 minutes. Stir and break up again. Repeat this fry-and-break cycle a total of three times. This controlled frying is what transforms the raw, pale egusi paste into the nutty, golden, deeply flavoured egusi that the soup is built around.
micro_steps: Add the entire egusi paste into the pot with the fried pepper base
Stir once to combine the egusi into the palm oil and pepper mixture
Reduce heat to medium and then stop stirring completely
Leave the egusi to sit undisturbed for 3 minutes — do not touch it
After 3 minutes, stir firmly and scrape up the crust from the bottom — break up any large lumps
Leave undisturbed again for 2 minutes
Stir and break up again — the egusi should be visibly changing from pale yellow to golden
Leave for 2 more minutes
Stir and break up one final time — this completes the fry-and-break cycle
The egusi should now be golden, smell toasty and nutty, and be forming small, firm golden-brown lumps
steps.sensory_cue: As the egusi fries, it visibly changes from pale yellow-white to a deeper golden colour. The smell shifts from raw, slightly bland ground seeds to something nutty, toasty, and rich — like roasting melon seeds. Small, golden-brown irregular lumps forming throughout the mixture is exactly the right result. The pot will smell extraordinary at this point.
steps.failure_risk: Constant stirring is the enemy of good egusi — it prevents the contact with the hot surface that creates the flavour. The controlled fry-and-break method is specifically what develops the nutty, toasted depth that separates exceptional egusi soup from mediocre versions. Trust the process and resist the urge to keep stirring.

steps.title: Add Stock and Cook the Egusi
steps.instruction: With the egusi properly fried and golden, begin adding the meat stock — add it gradually, one cup at a time, stirring after each addition and allowing the egusi to absorb the liquid before adding more. The egusi will swell and the soup will thicken quickly. Add enough stock to achieve a thick, rich consistency — the soup should look dense and coating, not watery. Add the fried meat, stockfish, and flaked smoked fish, stirring gently with a folding motion to avoid breaking the fish apart. Crush and add the remaining seasoning cubes, stir in, and bring the entire pot to a steady simmer over medium heat.
micro_steps: Pour 1 cup of meat stock into the pot and stir it through the fried egusi
Let the egusi absorb the stock for 1 minute before assessing the consistency
Add the second cup of stock and stir in — the soup should now look thick, rich, and coating
If the soup looks too thick after 2 cups, add a small splash more stock; if it looks right, stop
Add the fried meat pieces and fold in gently
Add the deboned stockfish and fold in carefully to keep the pieces intact
Add the flaked smoked fish last and fold in very gently
Crush the remaining seasoning cubes to powder and stir in
Bring to a steady simmer over medium heat
Stir once from the bottom and check the consistency — the soup should be thick and the egusi lumps fully coated
steps.sensory_cue: The soup at this stage should look thick and deeply coloured — the rich orange-red of the palm oil and pepper base with the golden egusi lumps distributed throughout. The meat and fish should be half-submerged in the thick base. The aroma should be full, layered, and complex — palm oil, crayfish, egusi, and smoked fish all present at once.
steps.failure_risk: Adding all the stock at once before the egusi has finished frying results in pale, gummy, partially raw egusi with no depth of flavour — the liquid stops the frying process mid-way. Always add stock only after the egusi has gone through the full fry-and-break cycle and is golden. Add it gradually and let the egusi absorb each addition before deciding if more is needed.

steps.title: Simmer, Season and Add Leaves
steps.instruction: Cover the pot and simmer on low heat for 20 minutes, lifting the lid to stir gently every 5 minutes. The egusi will fully absorb the stock, the soup will thicken further, and all the flavours will knit together into something cohesive and deeply complex. Taste at the 15-minute mark and add salt carefully — the smoked fish and stockfish have already contributed significant saltiness. Add your chosen leaf — spinach, pumpkin leaf (ugwu), or pre-washed and squeezed bitter leaf — stir it in and cook uncovered for a final 5 minutes only. The leaf should wilt and turn deep green, not go grey or slimy.
micro_steps: Cover the pot and reduce heat to low
Simmer for 5 minutes then stir gently from the bottom — replace the lid
Repeat every 5 minutes for a total simmering time of 20 minutes
At the 15-minute mark, taste the soup — assess the salt level carefully
Add salt in small pinches only, tasting between each addition
If using bitter leaf, ensure it has been washed and squeezed thoroughly before adding
Add your chosen leaf to the pot and stir in
Remove the lid and increase heat slightly to medium-low
Cook uncovered for exactly 5 minutes, stirring once halfway through
The leaves should be wilted and deep green — not grey, not slimy
Do a final taste and adjust salt one last time if needed
Turn off heat and serve immediately with swallow of choice
steps.sensory_cue: Finished egusi soup should be thick, glossy, and deeply orange-red with golden egusi lumps throughout. The egusi lumps should be fully cooked — golden, nutty, and firm but not hard or dry. The leaves should be bright, vivid green and just wilted. One taste should deliver all the layers at once — sweetness from the palm oil, heat from the pepper, nuttiness from the egusi, and deep umami from the crayfish and fish.
steps.failure_risk: Overcooking the leaves beyond 5 minutes turns them grey, slimy, and unpleasantly bitter — especially bitter leaf, which becomes aggressive and overwhelming when overcooked. Add leaves only at the very end and watch the time carefully. If using bitter leaf, thorough washing and squeezing before adding is non-negotiable — underwashed bitter leaf will make the entire soup unpalatably bitter.
